speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Renee Firestone in Los Angeles on August 4, 1994.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is roll three. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: When you found out that the forest is gone, where did you go from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went back to Rowne, Rowne. Over there was-- over-- [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] Anyway, in-- in Rowne, I find even a few family what they was from my town. Yeah. They was [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. The father, the man, died in the grave, and the mother and-- and three children came out. So I find them in Rowne. So I was with them. But I couldn't-- she used to [INAUDIBLE] make some-- [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] in-- in-- to-- to make money, to [NON-ENGLISH]. I couldn't do nothing. I can't do it. I can't-- I was so miserable, so I can't.

speaker: interviewee
text: Later, I came-- she was taking over there, was staying on-- all-- also Polish when they sold something. You see? And I take a look in the-- in the tables. They have [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], and they make this for-- for paper. Whatever they sold, they-- they they put it in the paper. I couldn't do it. I couldn't stay over there, and I couldn't look at what they make from-- from our-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I-- I went to Poland. Then in Rowne came Haganah boys. And they took us over from-- from Ukraine. We have to go. So they took us to Poland. So I was in Lublin, the first was, Lublin. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then in-- in-- in Lodz.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Lodz, I find a nephew from my brothers. I got three brothers. My brothers, two sons was in the Russia army. So one killed in the front. One is alive. And when we came to Lodz, we have to write our name was a [? command. ?] And I wrote-- we wrote name and I wrote my maiden name. And in-- in a few weeks, they called me from-- they called me from the-- the committee. And they told me that yesterday was here a boy, a soldier, and-- and he told that here is Kulish and over there is Kulish.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he was born over there in-- in Russia. He don't know me, and I don't know him. So [NON-ENGLISH]. So-- so they told them, tomorrow morning, they told him, come in, and she will be here, I told him. I came in. I don't know him. I don't know. So he told me that he remembered his father and mother told him that they are in Ukraine and here and so on, all the relatives. So he don't know because it was five sisters. The told him [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] is alive. Oh, but [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. So then, they-- they-- I came in. I-- I told them, I-- I am [INAUDIBLE] daughter. [INAUDIBLE]. So-- so he was there in Lodz. In-- in Lodz, he find a-- a nice girl. And I was Hungarian rabbi. I called the rabbi, and he gave them [NON-ENGLISH], and they married in Lodz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Later, he told me, you don't go now to Israel. Israel will be a war. I have to go. I have to help to make our-- our country, too help. And he was a soldier in the Russian. He-- he knows. Even when he came, I saw him with the-- with the Russian [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].

speaker: interviewee
text: So he went to Israel. And he was in-- and-- and I was over, and I went to Pocking. From-- from Lodz, the Haganah boys took us over to Pocking. No, I-- I-- I came to Bayern before Pocking. I forgot, yeah. To Bayern, this was before Pocking. Later that was.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bayern, when we came, maybe around 50 Jewish people, mostly women, mostly. A few men, was. And in Bayern, over there was also some camps, some little camps over there. But was all very full. When we come in the-- in that 50, we meet over there [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Eisenhower. General Eisenhower. General Eisenhower, we was without stockings, without shoes, and was raining over there, terrible. So General was talking. We didn't understand. So came up another chaplain, and he told me, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. I am want from you. And the general want-- the-- the-- the soldiers will make such a tents, and you go in. You know, in the middle is a-- a stick. You know, we-- when we go-- we come in, it fall the rain. It was so raining. And we-- we-- we were standing. But the General Eisenhower then-- then he was a general. Later was president. So he told us that he will very soon, he will try to take us out. He-- we was over there about four, five weeks in Bayern, in the tent. And then we come to-- to Pocking Waldstadt. And Pocking Waldstadt, I was over two years. I was over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did you come to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So my nephew with his wife, they left to Israel. And they write to me, to Pocking, they write to me that don't come to Israel, because will be a-- a war. And you got enough, and we don't want you to suffer anymore. You try or Canada or America. I cannot write to them. So-- so it happened that the-- the Jewish Joint, a paper was. Two ladies from St. Louis, Missouri, two sister lost. One lost-- one lost a son. In-- when-- when Japan was coming after, one lost a son and one lost a son-in-law. They took-- they took two-- they got two brothers, the two sister-in-laws, elderly people. And she want to take from the camps, take out a-- a woman. So-- so it happened, and she gave me the paper. She gave me the paper, and-- and-- and they start writing to-- to the Joint. They start writing that they want connect with me, and they will send me something. They were sending me every week. Every week-- I will show you a card-- a picture what I make of [INAUDIBLE] that I got. I came over there dressed with a hat, with a-- a coat which I-- I bought in Germany. Because they send me cigarettes, and I don't smoke never. They send me chocolate, and I don't need it, so I sold this, and I made-- I make myself.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I came to St. Louis, Missouri. And I was over there, two elder the ladies. Of course, they are-- one is a-- is their son, he was-- he was writing that he's-- if I wouldn't be-- I-- he would-- he would help me if I-- so but I-- in-- in a few weeks, I start asking their children, please give me something to work. So they took me to a Yiddish man, a [NON-ENGLISH]. They was in school together. And-- and-- so, and they let me over there. A nurse's uniforms I was wearing. [INAUDIBLE] I was working errands also. The [INAUDIBLE] Company, you know, nurses' uniforms. So when did you remarry?

speaker: interviewee
text: You-- you-- oh, this is a big story. I married in-- in-- I married in St. Louis. The man was in Pocking with me. He-- he was in Auschwitz. And so he came, and he was in Pocking when I was. But I-- I told him-- he came over. You see, women was separate and the men separate. He came over. I told him, I even don't think. I wouldn't do such a thing. I lost so much. I will go-- And I-- I forget her name. He went to America, I know, because it's-- where-- where he lived was across the door. So I know he got a sister in-- in Los Angeles. So he went to Los Angeles. His sister took him out. So-- and I didn't-- didn't-- even-- but he went later. So-- yeah. Later, when-- if he is-- oh, boy. [INAUDIBLE]. Yeah. He was. And I-- and I, when I was in--

speaker: interviewer
text: St. Louis?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. When I came already St. Louis, I [INAUDIBLE]. I didn't-- I didn't know. When I came here, all of a sudden, in St. Louis, I-- I came to St. Louis with a ship with 50-- with 150, 140 people what went to America. And this is America-- American ship, took us.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the people with me, what they used to be with-- with-- with me and also in the-- but what they was on the ship. Some of them went to Los Angeles. They came in Los Angeles also from Pocking. They recognized him. So he start going-- I used to go-- my hair was so long, always. So he asked, don't you know there a Sonia with-- she used to go, you know. Sonia, she used to go with [INAUDIBLE]? [LAUGHS] So they told, she came with us. And to be maybe three, four weeks in St. Louis, I got a letter from him. And I-- I-- I made good money, and I-- I-- and I had a good-- a good room. Come with what-- I know you and you know me. He used to bring me, in Pocking, some water, some oats, you know? So he-- he wanted to help. I have here a picture if you want to see. I have here his picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, and I, in St. Louis, I got a-- a-- from our town a-- a couple. They was 90 years old when I came. And he was from our town. And we-- my father or my grandfather who don't know. It only shocked them [INAUDIBLE]. And-- and the town was like our own. Everybody knows everybody.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the-- the-- the-- the people, they-- they ask me if there are brothers. Their brothers was two brothers there, was killed the same when my-- when-- over there. So I told them, I was in school with your brothers. I was in a yeshiva with your-- with your-- with the girls, got a yeshiva girls. A yeshiva. And but-- nobody was alive. Everybody was gone. So what happened with this man?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this man, he sent me-- he sent to a rabbi, in-- to St. Louis to a rabbi. Mhm. See, they-- the-- the-- my landsman is going to the shul, and the rabbi told him somebody sent me a letter, and I-- I don't know a Louis-- Louis Green. Oh, they know me here, Green. I came, I was Green. Is--

speaker: interviewee
text: So know-- I-- I know where all this is, so I told the landsman this and this. He couldn't catch me over there, so he catch me here. Here, I-- so I need where-- a little home, a little--

speaker: interviewee
text: And so it happened. So I told the landsman, no, I would answer, and I don't want. I want to-- I will see what is well.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he told me, no. Give it to me. I will answer, good? You only write your name. So I write him the name, and he wrote the letter. And in the letter, I-- he write, he shall come to him. He-- he invited him. So he came to St. Louis. Can I get up and take the picture? I'm want to show it-- no. Later.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he went to St. Louis. He came and was a few weeks. But-- but the landsman, I was [INAUDIBLE] still. And he worked with the landsman. And-- and a-- and a few weeks, they made a house. His daughters live-- lived in-- in-- not in St. Louis, but near St. Louis. They made over there a house, and he came here. But my dear, they knew he was-- he was [INAUDIBLE] in the morning. And in the evening, we left for Los Angeles. When-- when we was in the train with the train, and we was in the train. He told me I didn't told you something. I have to tell you. And please, please, have [NON-ENGLISH]. And I didn't tell you. He told me in a [INAUDIBLE]. He told me that he was in Auschwitz under Mengele. That Mengele, he wasn't Mengele and Mengele did what he-- he was doing.

words.text: a house, and he came here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Experimented.

speaker: interviewee
text: That we can't have any children. I-- I-- I-- then I-- I was taken. So before we-- he asked me about the children, my-- and this came up. So then he told me. So I didn't know. I was to go with him or to go away. I decided when God take-- take from me, away the children, maybe I am not-- maybe I-- maybe I did something what I am not wanted to have. And I was going with him. I told him, don't cry. And I wouldn't cry. And the two of us will go. This was till he died. I came in here. Yeah. So then we-- I married here. And I was 19 years with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Mr. Pepper is your third husband. The third husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you married him--

speaker: interviewee
text: Here in the shul, in this shul, yeah. Yeah. Yeah, his daughter used to came very often to the synagogue, and he used to come. His-- so I-- I know him a little from the synagogue. I never talked to him till his daughter came up, when we finished the davening-- please come with me. I want to talk to you. So he came over here on the bench, and we talked. And she told me. And I-- I told her that I don't think, or I don't-- but tomorrow morning he was over. I was living in the Weinberg, in another building. He came over once in the morning, in the morning and afternoon. [LAUGHS] Sonia, will you introduce your family to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband, Harry Pepper. Our lovely daughter, Barbara [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Barbara is Harry's daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Barbara, how do you feel, at this time, getting a mother who's a survivor of the Holocaust? BARBARA (STEP-DAUGHTER): It's probably the most privileged, most beautiful thing that I could ever want in our lives, to have someone so very special like Sonny in our home. It's wonderful, you know, to have her as an extension of all of us. They-- they've come through so much, and the story is finally going to be told. It's a story that we've wanted told for an awful long time. And-- and it's-- we're just so very happy that it's-- it's finally going to be done. You're a lucky man, Harry. BARBARA (STEP-DAUGHTER): And a very happy moment in our lives, that we have her. How do-- how do you feel, at-- at this age?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] . Well, it's kind of--

speaker: interviewer
text: You have to speak louder, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. It's [INAUDIBLE] it's really wonderful to have [INAUDIBLE] to tell what's going on with [INAUDIBLE]. Because some people think it never happens anymore. So that's a good idea to have it there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sonia, do you have any message for future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: What-- what can we say? We don't want such a thing to happen. We don't want this. And we have to hope the future will be better and better. We hope to God that he wouldn't do such a thing, but it was. It was horrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you talk to your grandchildren about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, oh, yeah. We love them very much. She-- she has already two great-- two grandchildren. He has to great-grandchildren. Very often, yeah. We got them-- her son and daughter-in-law were here two weeks, three weeks ago, for dinner with us. They-- they come often. Yeah, we see them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I just want to thank all three of you for joining us. And I understand you have some pictures--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I have a few--

speaker: interviewee
text: That you would like to show. BARBARA (STEP-DAUGHTER): I have them. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taking in Pocking Waldstadt with so many other refugees that I can't [INAUDIBLE]. Pocking in '47. This is me. I am Sonia Pepper. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is my father. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I-- my nephew gave it to me, the picture. In-- in-- in '48, he gave me the picture, my father. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Yone Kulish. Here, this is my second husband. His name is Louis Green. And he's not here anymore. He died, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Start again.

speaker: interviewee
text: I start again?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I don't know when the picture was taken. In Auschwitz. I don't know. This is my husband. His name is Louis Green. The picture was when he was liberated. So somebody gave-- gave him the picture. And-- and he was under Mengele.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Auschwitz. Here is the same, my second husband, Louis Green. And here in the United States, he was a cantor in the-- in a synagogue. This is me, Sonia Pepper. And whatever I go here, so the good people from St. Louis send me. I bought a coat and a hat and a pocketbook, and I came to St. Louis, Missouri.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: So there's me, Sonia Pepper, with my second husband, Louis Green. And here we are at a bar mitzvah. And in '53, in 1953, here in Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's me, Sonia Pepper. I am by the beach. Oh, this is 1963. Los Angeles. My-- it's me, Sonia Pepper, my husband Harry Pepper. Oh, and our lovely daughter made the picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in--

speaker: interviewee
text: BARBARA (STEP-DAUGHTER): That was the wedding dress that you wore in your wedding here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where was taken? Here in Los Angeles. In Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that your wedding picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this is not-- is it our wedding picture? I don't-- so when we got married, in '87. Yeah, wedding picture in '87. Me, Sonia Pepper with my husband, Harry Pepper. And we took the picture in the synagogue last year, here in the synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Last year. Last year.

speaker: interviewee
text: '93. Oh, the '93, I think that was.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah? Yeah. This my family now. And I like them very, very much. Oh, this was made in '94. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Dora's two sons. Yeah, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: BARBARA (STEP-DAUGHTER): Three sons. And two grandchildren, great-grandchildren. BARBARA (STEP-DAUGHTER): Three sons.

speaker: interviewer
text: Three sons.
